speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four. You were talking about your sister Laura.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES] The next morning, we did not go to work because we did not know if it's safe. Suddenly, even though we thought that our cellar, being so in the rear, that nobody knows, my brother [INAUDIBLE] is standing downstairs, and suddenly, from the window, he sees a rifle is being thrust in. Thrust in. And a German said, get out! Get out! And my brother said, don't shoot. Don't shoot. There was a Ukrainian policeman who was-- he told us he's collecting some-- gathering some people that they have to help to clean up. He did not say what. When we were up on that-- under the rooftop there, my brother had high temperature. He was sick. We had no aspirin, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So taking him would have been terrible. We started begging. And we know men are in bigger danger than women. So my sister and I said, take us. We're going to go help clean up. Hesitantly, he agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are both walking, with my Laura-- my sister and I. And he brings us to the area where our synagogue was, Beth Olam [INAUDIBLE] synagogue, and walks us in-- he leads us into that place, and he said, stay here until I get back. When we got used to the darkness, we saw bodies on the floor stretched out like-- like sardines. It was frightening to be so close to a dead bodies. And then we saw there was-- in Brody, there was an orphanage. And it was a husband and wife that they were-- there was the adopted fathers in charge, Mr. And Mrs. Okser. Very respectable people. And now we saw Mrs. Okser shot. And her dress was up that you saw her thigh naked. It's like-- my sister felt it's like indignant in death. So she found a rag and covered her. It was gruesome to be near dead bodies. And we wanted to run. But if we run, the guard knows where he got us from. So he's going to come. So we have to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we embraced each other, and we cried. And then there was one fellow there, which-- he was shot. And my sister recognized that was Shia. She went to school with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, like, lined up bodies. What they needed is men to carry out the bodies, or wherever places they were shot for that. We were frightened and crying, thinking that we're going to be killed, too. Then that guard came down with a group of Jews, group of men. And he said, we have enough men for work. Go home. We did not believe that maybe as we walk he's going to shoot after us. So we ran crying, crying. And we came home. When my parents saw us, the joy and the tear. And when we told them all that, what's happening, we were very depressed. And we're very-- we had no more illusions. Seeing, really at close range, the dead people, we had no illusions that even if the Allies come close knocking at the door, it would be too late for us. I was-- the following say, I was going back to Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And my brothers went back. I did not have to explain. He knew. He was a patriotic German. He was not a Nazi. But sometime he would say, if the war is about to be lost, we are now in the process of developing a weapon which will shock the world. It seems that they had had meetings where they discussed that they were in the process of nuclear weapon to have. The Germans were, in the beginning, developing it. And we're going to have it soon. If we go down, the whole world will go down. Sometimes he would-- in the cabinet he had bread and butter. He would take a slice of bread. He would put a lot of butter, and tell me to sit down and eat. He was a kind man, but stern. [PAUSES] And he did not ask question why I did not come, because he knew what's going on in the city. [? Mrs. Schubert ?] felt at that safe, because being in a building where there are Germans, it wouldn't occur to them that there's a Jewish there occupying. And when I came to work, when I finished, [? Mrs. Schubert ?] and I, we were talking. And we knew who was taken away. Who was shot. And all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she kept saying, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. How long can-- when is-- what's happening to our people? What's going to happen? And so.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mrs. Schubert-- ?] the German-- there was one officer, high-ranking, from the kommandantur there. He had a radio, and it was in a German station. But when she was polishing his rooms, polishing the floor-- and I helped her-- it was facing the front. So I was able to stand by the window. If anybody would come, I could see coming. And by the time they get up, she would turn on-- she'd remember the station that she started from, turn on London [INAUDIBLE] from London in German. And they were telling about the defeats and conquests of their-- the-- the Germans are being beaten at Stalingrad, Leningrad. They are suffering defeats. How many dead. And it seems-- and then they played the national anthem. It was optimistic. And soon as we finished, she put it back to that same station. Not that that I was such intellectual at that time, but I gathered to and wrote down whatever information I had and brought it home. And through our friends that sometimes snuck into our house, they used to come to our house for that little bit of information, because even for the Christian population, having a radio was very hazardous at that time. So I brought some hope. Sometimes the German defeats were so great that we thought that they-- that the liberators are knocking at our door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1940-- 40-- 43.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was '43 already?

speaker: interviewer
text: So had the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: --been established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a minute. Just a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto was established in December of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [INAUDIBLE] was in '42. It's-- I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just wanted to clarify.

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42-- I-- it's very hard to put it in order--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but we were living in a cellar, so we were not in a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr.-- an incident happened, of which I never forget. Mr. [INAUDIBLE], when he came home in the afternoon, he would lie down and read his paper. And he would send me to the military kitchen to bring his dinner. [PAUSES] And at one time, he felt like having beer. It was a soldatenheim, a German canteen within walking distance. I should go with a pitcher and bring him some beer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to fetch the beer for him. But I am not allowed to go into that place without my arm band, because that was life-threatening. And I walk in. The-- the-- that canteen was occupied. It was a movie theater before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I walked in, I saw Polish-- pretty Polish girls, waitresses, serving them drinks. And there was a counter with those kegs of beer where he's pouring. So I approached it, and reached with my pitcher, and showed him a note from the German that he should give me a beer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When that German saw my star of David, something happened to him, like a wild animal. He did not go around the counter, but jumped across the counter, and pushed me against the wall, where I fell. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You cursed Jews! What are you doing in a soldatenheim? What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I tried to tell him that I'm-- I'm bringing-- fetching a beer for the German, he slapped me. And I felt my face like throbbing. Hold your mouth. Don't talk. And he went back-- and I-- my jug for beer was on the counter. And I'm standing [PAUSES] in fear, tears coming down. And he sadistically enjoys my standing there. And he's not giving me the beer.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a German soldier there. He said, what satisfaction do you get in hitting that girl? Making her suffer? You see that she's fetching the beer for the German. So give her the beer if you don't want to see her, and let her go.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, let her suffer. And after a while, he fetched-- he poured the beer, and the German gave it to me. And I ran through the street with that pitcher of beer that the foam should not go down. Running, and my face throbbing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came upstairs, [? Mrs. Schubert ?] opened the door for me. She said, what happened? What happened to you? I said, I'll tell you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into Mr. [INAUDIBLE] room. He was reading the paper. He didn't look up. I poured beer in a glass for him. And the rest I left on the night table near him. And I came into the kitchen to Mrs. [? Schubert, ?] and I told her what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, dear, God, how long are you going to punish us? How long are we going to suffer? [PAUSES] And [PAUSES] after, in the afternoon, I went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: When Mr.-- Mr. [INAUDIBLE] was away, we watched. I brought the news. And people came to us. The next-- after a few days, we heard rumors. We heard the rumors that there's going to be a roundup-- [? aktion, ?] they call it-- a roundup of Jews. We already knowing what happened, the killing and the shooting, from the previous roundup. We already knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sitting in our cellar there, we did not know if it's safe. So, again, we thought, are we going to go up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we thought we might not be lucky this time, because they're going to go have a more thorough search. And then we thought, perhaps we'll go into the woods, because the ghetto was not created then. So we are free to go. At night, we'll go to the woods and wait in the woods. What was-- my father's sister, Rachel, went to the woods with her children. She was dealing, like pedaling goods and things. And she dealt with farmers, so she knew some. She went to a farm. And she had some-- saved some materials that she had. She figured, maybe they're going to hide our-- find a hiding place for her and the children. But the farmer had a small place with children that he said they wouldn't be safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had a son. Also his name was Ziggy, my cousin, the oldest son. And he said he's going to dig a hiding-- a bunker, big, and remove the soil, dig in. He dug it, like, six feet, seven feet by four that they could bury themself there and stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the-- at night, the farmer would let them in, and they could wash and eat. And they'll pay him. And they can trust him. It was 20 kilometers from our house, very remote in the forest. The houses were scattered. So they felt they had a way out. When we heard-- one time my father is walking on the ruins, walking, like, through the ruins outside. And he sees a peasant looking around on our-- in our-- where the backyard was. And my father approached him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked, do you know where [INAUDIBLE] house was? Because he-- she described where our house was. He said, I am [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I have a message from your sister that if there is any danger, her son has built-- has dug out a place that you could come there and hide with us. So he delivered the message. And somehow we didn't think of it. But when this knowledge that through our-- through a definite source that there's going to be a roundup, they call it [? aktion, ?] this and this day, we thought, maybe we'll go there. We should go there. It was in the wintertime. We were all bundled up. And walked, and we don't know direction, but we know there was a track that a train was passing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finally-- the snow was knee-high, but then we found that the tracks are clean when the train are passing. We walk on the tracks. If we hear a train coming, we run off.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked a long time at night. And we got tired, because we're all bundled up. And my mother said, let's sit down. She couldn't walk anymore. We were cold, stiff. We were sitting a few minutes. My father said, if you sit any longer, we'll freeze to death. And you wouldn't [INAUDIBLE] to go up. Let's walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got up again, And we walked and walked. And she described that at the end of the track, we approach a forest. And there's a path there. And then we'll see, like, a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were frozen and tired. And it took a long time to walk at night. It was a moonlit night. So we were-- and the snow reflects. It was not so dark. And we heard dogs barking. And we were afraid if a farmer will come out, they'll find us. We're afraid. But we don't know which barn. And this is a way-- we see another barn. If we expose ourself to other Christians, we're afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going close to the wall, listening. And suddenly we heard murmur, and listening. And my brother said, I think they're speaking Yiddish. So we walked to the main entrance, and we opened the door. There was a petroleum lamp lit there on the table, dim lights. A room full of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came in, and my aunt, when she saw us, she came running towards us. We were all frozen, exhausted. We dropped to the ground. She helped us take off the boots. She made us hot tea. And we thought that we are safe. But when we see so many people, they're bound to be discovered. She said, when she first came, it was just them. At night, they would go in. But the farmer got smart. He could make money. So people came. And they pay him daily. They pay him, so he makes money. And it's not safe anymore. And that is why they have that dugout bunker. She said, in the daytime, tomorrow morning-- we-- we-- from walking, we were exhausted. We fell asleep. Tomorrow morning, when nobody sees, we're going to go into that bunker. And that's where we're going to stay till dark. It's safer there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, we paid for it, what the farmer made. Hot-- something hot to drink, and bread with butter. And we walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the snow so fluffy and smooth. He had a branch on his hands where he covered our tracks, and brought us to a place where there were, like, a bush. But this was a camouflage. Underneath there was a boards-- nailed boards. This was the cover. He slide it away, and we had to, like, let ourselves down into a-- into that underground hole. We went in. It was like above our head a little. For me-- I mean, for me, it was higher. But for my brother, he's taller, so it was crouched down. And there was, like, from the dirt, made, like, a bench. We were sitting. There was bedding inside. And then he slide it, and the branches covered it. And we were sitting there. Dark. Pitch dark. You cannot see. it's like-- in a grave it has to be like that, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're sitting. And we wouldn't-- and how are we going to know when it's over? How are we going to know when it's daylight?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew what time it is, so periodically, after several hours, he would slide a little, look at the watch. We're sitting. It's still daylight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, we felt things crawling on our faces, like an itch. Crawling. What's crawling on us? Crawling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were lice. I guess when you don't have good nutrition and when you don't wash, and when you're in a concentrated area, lice, like, biting us. We felt itching and crawling by the eyes. It was all so terrible. And there was not much air. There was a little slide where it had a little air coming. It's like buried alive. We're sitting. Keep doing. And that bedding was damp-ish. It had a stench. I think it was better to die than to live like that. And when darkness came, he slide it open. Again, with that branch, he was sweeping. We shouldn't show tracks. We came in. We did not-- we-- the-- the farmer sent his son to fetch the horse and wagon to go to town and see what's doing. Bring us information.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went to town, and it was quiet. Nothing was taking place. There were no rounding up of Jews. Was that man-- that policeman who told us wrong? So we thought, maybe it's tomorrow. We may as well stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: We dreaded, in the daytime, going back into that hole with the lice. But we went back, and then we came out. Then the farmer's son came back. He says, it's quiet in the city. There is no roundup. It was a false alarm. We had no facilities to be there. The farmer would not give us. All the people started scattering, going. And we felt it's safer to go separately. We shouldn't be seen in a group going back, going home. We bid goodbye to our Aunt, Rachel, and my cousin Ziggy, and [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], her boy. We kissed them, and thanked them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were on our way back, constantly feeling that we're covered with lice. My father, when he kissed his sister saying goodbye, he was crying on the way home, my sister is buried alive. Eaten by lice. Is-- how long is it going to last? How long can she survive like that? Who knows when the war's going to end? But We thought that there's no future for us there. So we approached our cellar. We walked. We came back to our cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't go in right away. My mother got in and took water. And we washed on the outside, stripped and washed on the outside, even though it was cold. And then when we get in, she warmed the water. We washed, and put out our clothes to freeze, because it was winter. Maybe that would kill the lice. And my father cried, my sister is buried alive. Living-- living-- waiting for death there. But we were wiser people. We knew that there's no-- we cannot run. Two days-- the Germans tricked us. The freight trains were ready. Two days later-- because they knew Jews are scattered. As long as no ghetto, people scatter and run. This way, they come back. Early in the morning, we heard shots. [PAUSES] We woke up, and we knew that an [? aktion ?] is in process. We're like shivering. We hurriedly-- hurriedly dressed ourselves. We climbed up under the rooftop-- and it was cold. It was winter already by then-- lying there, waiting, and hearing shooting and boys running. And we heard groups of people that they were leading them. Probably beyond our neighborhood at those [INAUDIBLE] some people lived. And we heard them crying. And in Yiddish, Shema Israel, crying. They were leading them to the trains. And we survived. But when a siren-- then there's a siren, a loud siren, announcing that they met their quota. Now we can come out. And we waited again. The next day I go back to work, and we waited for the inevitable to come. There's no hope. I was at Mrs. [? Schubert's-- ?] and she had many acquaintances with farmers. They had wholesale businesses, and so on. One time I was finished cleaning Mr. [INAUDIBLE] room. I'm coming-- coming into the kitchen, and I see some gentiles, some men, sitting there talking. I withdrew myself, because it's private. She said, no, come in. Come in. They were sitting discussing. She was purchasing from them false Christian documents. They were [? Volks ?] [? German, ?] which means Germans-- Poles of German descent. Let's say the father was German. So they-- being that they are part German, they have in-- office positions. And when the Christians announced a new registration of the Christian population, new documents will be issued. Therefore, separate them completely from the Jews. And he was in that office where people had to-- people bring-- had [INAUDIBLE] ID photograph, and they had forms, give them your information. And when they stamped that document, half of it was on the photograph, and half on the document. This way, you couldn't take a false-- a document and paste your picture on. There is no stamp on it. This way, it's bona fide that this is the real document.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were making money. They had access to those blank forms where the Christian people bring the photographs and give them all the information filling out. What could stop them from smuggling in Jewish photographs? And then, when it goes further to the hand where-- the one that stamps them-- stamp them?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a group of them. They had an occasion to make money. But money didn't have much value, just valuable things. And [? Mrs. Schubert ?] was not poor. And a friend of hers, Mrs. Rosenfelt, also had. So for the two of-- two families they obtain-- they were obtaining documents. She'll have to supply a photograph, give them information.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she listed what she could give them. They agreed. And now she said, how about you? You should have a document. You don't look typically Jewish. I said, [? Mrs. Schubert, ?] our house burned down. We couldn't save much. We have five children. What does my father have to give? She said to them, fellas, she doesn't look Jewish. So she has a chance. Could you help her? Could you be more reasonable?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, when the time comes that she's ready, we're going to talk. They went away, because it takes time till the documents, till all that, is processed. [PAUSES] In the meantime, I came home and just casual told my father about it. And we knew that we are doomed. We knew it's just a matter of time. No more illusions. No more hope. And I told my father that [? Mrs. Schubert ?] have-- this fellow's name was Jacques. He was a Volksdeutsche, which means of German descent. And he is issuing false Christian documents. And he's giving out birth certificates, like, from the church; and an arbeitskarte, a card that she is working; identification card, as a work pass for a card; and several documents, very convincing. And she has to have-- the photograph has a stamp from the German kommandantur, which half is on the stamp, and half is on the document. Nobody could have done it, so it's very convincing.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father listened. The next night when I came home, he said, how much? What should I have to give? I said, I will-- Laura does not look Jewish. I don't look Jewish. Maybe-- men, they are circumcised. They could examine them. They could betray.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody-- my father said, somebody should survive. We thought there'll be no Jews in Europe. Somebody should survive to tell what was done to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said-- he wants all the information I should give him. I said, Daddy, we lost everything from the fire. We don't have anything to give. And what you give to Mr. [INAUDIBLE], a few things, when we'll be starving to survive? You have to have that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said-- he was thinking somebody should survive. Find out. Find out. He wanted somebody to live. And-- and he-- before the war, my Laura joined a Zionist organization, [? Hashomer ?] [? Hatzair. ?] And then it was necessary to go to a Hachshara, they call it, because Palestine was a pioneer country where they were-- had to meet, farm, and develop the country. So they needed to learn trades and farming, so they all had Hachshara where they would go to other towns and work, and get experience. Work for very little pay. And the farmers were glad to give them that employment, because they didn't pay that much. And they had, like, a commune life to get used to the life in Israel where it's a commune life and you have to share.
